perturbation.original_text: Green Cross shall Commercialize the Products in the Territory under the worldwide brand specified by MacroGenics ("Product Brand"), except to the extent such branding is not permitted by any applicable Regulatory Authority, or deemed culturally inappropriate, in the Territory, in which case MacroGenics shall specify an alternate Product Brand.
perturbation.changed_text: Green Cross shall Commercialize the Products in the Territory under a brand of its choosing. MacroGenics may suggest a Product Brand.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes MacroGenics' control over the product brand in South Korea. Under the Trademark Act of South Korea, unauthorized use of a registered trademark can lead to legal consequences. MacroGenics relinquishing control over the brand creates potential liability for Green Cross if they select a brand that infringes on existing South Korean trademarks. This omission directly contradicts consumer protection laws related to trademark and branding compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the requirement for Green Cross to use the brand specified by MacroGenics, the contract increases the risk that Green Cross might inadvertently infringe on an existing trademark in South Korea, violating Article 66. The Korean Trademark Act protects brand owners from unauthorized use of their trademarks.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 37 CFR § 7.26 - Filing date of extension of protection for purposes of examination in the Office. CFR prev | next § 7.26 Filing date of extension of protection for purposes of examination in the Office . (a) If a request for extension of protection of an international registration to the United States is made in an international application and the request includes a declaration of a bona fide intention to use the mark in commerce as set out in § 2.33(e) of this chapter, the filing date of the extension of protection to the United States is the international registration date. (b) If a request for extension of protection of an international registration to the United States is made in a subsequent designation and the request includes a declaration of a bona fide intention to use the mark in commerce as set out in § 2.33(e) , the filing date of the extension of protection to the United States is the date that the International Bureau records the subsequent designation . perturbation.justification: The modified text removes MacroGenics' control over the product brand, potentially leading Green Cross to infringe on existing South Korean trademarks, which violates trademark laws. The original contract ensured compliance by specifying that MacroGenics would define the brand. The changed contract allows Green Cross to choose a brand with only a suggestion from MacroGenics, increasing the risk of trademark infringement. Although the scraped snippets provided are not directly relevant as one is missing and the other deals with US trademark filing dates, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation highlight the contradiction. The Omission of MacroGenics' brand control creates an omission legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Such amounts shall be determined from the books and records of Green Cross or its Related Party, maintained in accordance with International Financial Reporting Standards (IFRS) or such similar accounting principles, consistently applied. Green Cross further agrees, in determining such amounts, it will use Green Cross' then­current standard procedures and methodology, including Green Cross' then­current standard exchange rate methodology for the translation of foreign currency sales into U.S. Dollars or, in the case of Sublicensees, such similar methodology, consistently applied.
perturbation.changed_text: Such amounts shall be determined at Green Cross's discretion. Green Cross agrees, in determining such amounts, that it may use any procedures and methodology.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the IFRS compliance requirement and standard procedures for determining Net Sales, the contract creates a legal risk. South Korean law mandates financial transparency and accurate reporting for tax purposes. The omission may lead to misreporting, violating South Korean tax regulations.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts the South Korean Corporate Tax Act, Article 2, which implicitly requires financial records to be kept in a transparent and accurate manner. By allowing Green Cross to determine amounts at its discretion without adherence to specific standards (like IFRS) there is a potential violation. Companies must report financial figures honestly for tax purposes.

perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates adherence to IFRS or similar accounting principles, consistently applied, for determining amounts. It also requires the use of Green Cross' standard procedures and exchange rate methodology. The changed text removes these requirements, stating that amounts shall be determined at Green Cross's discretion and that Green Cross may use any procedures and methodology. The Perturbation Explanation states that by removing the IFRS compliance requirement and standard procedures, the contract creates a legal risk as it may lead to misreporting, violating South Korean tax regulations. The Law Explanation further emphasizes that this modification contradicts the South Korean Corporate Tax Act, Article 2, which implicitly requires financial records to be kept in a transparent and accurate manner and companies must report financial figures honestly for tax purposes. The omission of the IFRS compliance creates a legal risk.

perturbation.original_text: Green Cross agrees to indemnify and hold harmless MacroGenics, its Affiliates, and their directors, officers, employees and agents (individually and collectively, the "MacroGenics Indemnitee(s)") from and against all losses, liabilities, damages and expenses (including reasonable attorneys' fees and costs) incurred in connection with any claims, demands, actions or other proceedings by any Third Party (individually and collectively, "Losses") first arising after the Effective Date to the extent arising from (a) activities performed by Green Cross or any of its Affiliates or Permitted Subcontractors with respect to the research, Development, manufacture, use, Commercialization or sale of MGAH22 or Products or any other exercise of their rights or performance of their obligations hereunder, (b) the use by Green Cross or any of its Related Parties or Permitted Subcontractors of the MacroGenics Licensed Patents or MacroGenics Licensed Know-how except as permitted in this Agreement, (c) the negligence, illegal conduct or willful misconduct of Green Cross, or (d) Green Cross' material breach of this Agreement, except to the extent such Losses arise out of any of MacroGenics Indemnitee's negligence, illegal conduct or willful misconduct, or breach of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Green Cross shall indemnify MacroGenics for any direct damages arising from Green Cross's intentional misconduct related to the Commercialization of Products in South Korea.
perturbation.explanation: This change severely limits the scope of Green Cross's indemnification obligations. By restricting indemnification to "direct damages" and "intentional misconduct," and removing coverage for negligence and breach of contract, the revised clause fails to adequately protect MacroGenics from third-party claims, including product liability claims, arising from Green Cross's activities. This omission contradicts the principle of product liability under South Korean law, which holds manufacturers and distributors responsible for damages caused by defective products, even in the absence of intentional misconduct.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Product Liability Act of South Korea
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts the Product Liability Act of South Korea, Article 3, which outlines the responsibilities of manufacturers and distributors for harm caused by their products. The original indemnification clause provided broader protection. By limiting indemnification to only intentional misconduct and direct damages, MacroGenics is exposed to liabilities that Green Cross should arguably cover under general product liability principles in South Korea.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Coplay School Teachers Flood Board Meeting, Push for 'Fair Contract.'" While the full text can be found in the September 8, 2014, issue of The Morning Call , a synopsis is noted below. More than 100 of the Whitehall-Coplay School District's 286 teachers, nurses, psychologists and guidance counselors packed the school board meeting Monday to call for a new contract that would bring them bigger paychecks. The school board feels that what the teachers are asking is unreasonable, said Sultanik, the board's solicitor and negotiations spokesman. Sultanik said the board is proposing a fixed raise for each member of the Whitehall-Coplay Education Association. "So they all get raises, even though there are no salary increases," he said. "We're concerned that negotiations are not off to a good start," Sultanik said. The content of this article is intended to provide a general guide to the subject matter. Specialist advice should be sought about your specific circumstances. perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly limits Green Cross's indemnification obligations, focusing only on 'direct damages' from 'intentional misconduct.' This omission contradicts the principle of product liability, as manufacturers are generally held responsible for damages caused by defective products, even without intentional misconduct. The original text provided broader protection, covering negligence and breach of contract, whereas the modified text exposes MacroGenics to liabilities that Green Cross should arguably cover under general product liability principles. The law explanation emphasizes this point, aligning with the understanding that product liability extends beyond intentional actions.
